15: ho makarios kai monos dynamastes ho basileus ton basileonton kai kyrios ton kyrieonton (ho makarios kai monos dynastēs, ho basileus tōn basileuontōn kai kyrios tōn kyrieuontōn): 'the blessed and only Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords.' The doxology of 1 Timothy 6:15-16 applies to the epiphaneia of Jesus the titles that Hellenistic rulers and the Roman emperor claimed. Dynastēs (Sovereign/potentate) was used of divine-right rulers in the Hellenistic east. King of kings and Lord of lords echoes Deuteronomy 10:17 (YHWH as God of gods and Lord of lords) and Daniel 2:37, 47 (the God of heaven as King over kings). The doxology places the Roman emperor's pretensions under the sovereignty of Christ.
1: Hosoi eisin hypo zygon douloi (all who are under the yoke of slavery): hypo zygon (under the yoke) — an agricultural metaphor for the condition of the enslaved; the yoke evokes the heavy labor of draft animals. The instruction is addressed to slaves with non-believing masters (1 Timothy 6:1) and then with believing masters (1 Timothy 6:2), showing the community included both configurations. The rationale for respectful conduct is explicitly missional: so that to onoma tou theou kai hē didaskalia mē blasphēmētai (God's name and the teaching may not be slandered). The gospel's credibility in the wider Greco-Roman society is the reason for the social conduct requirement.
2: Mē kataphroneitsōsan hoti adelphoi eisin, alla mallon douleuetsōsan hoti pistoi eisin kai agapētoi hoi tēs euergesias antilambanos (let them not despise them because they are brothers, but serve them more fully because those who receive the benefit are faithful and beloved): the instruction counterintuitively does not say 'brothers are equals, so the slave relationship dissolves' but 'brothers are even more worthy of full service.' Euergesia (benefit, good service) and antilambanos (receiving, taking hold of) — the benefactor vocabulary suggests that the believing master's gracious reception of service should motivate increased diligence rather than exploitation of the relationship.
3: Ei tis heterodidaskalei kai mē proserchetai hygiainousi logois tois tou Kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou (if anyone teaches otherwise and does not agree with the sound words of our Lord Jesus Christ): heterodidaskalei (teaches otherwise/different things) — the same verb as 1 Timothy 1:3; the false teacher is defined negatively by deviation from apostolic teaching. Hygianousin logois (sound/healthy words) — hygianō (to be healthy) applied to teaching is characteristic of the Pastoral Epistles (1 Timothy 1:10; 1 Timothy 6:3; Titus 1:9; 1 Timothy 2:1; 2 Timothy 1:13; 1 Timothy 4:3); teaching is either healthy or diseased. Kata eusebeian (according to godliness) — the standard against which teaching is measured is practical godliness, not abstract correctness.
4: Tetyphōtai, mēden epistamenos, alla nosōn peri zētēseis kai logomachias (he is conceited, knowing nothing, but being diseased about controversies and word-battles): tetyphōtai (is conceited/has been made smoke-blind) — the perfect passive of typhōō (to cloud with smoke/vapor); the false teacher is intellectually blinded by self-inflation. Nosōn (being diseased/sick) — noseō extended from physical illness to intellectual-moral disorder; the false teacher is pathologically attracted to controversies. Logomachias (word-battles) — a hapax legomenon in the NT; logomachia = battles over words with no substantive content.
5: Porizmon einai tēn eusebeian (godliness as a means of profit/livelihood): porizmos (profit, financial gain, means of livelihood) — a commercial term; the false teachers have turned religious activity into a revenue stream. The charge of mercenary religion is a standard polemical accusation in the Greco-Roman world (Cynics, sophists, and Jewish teachers were all accused of teaching for money). Paul's charge is not that the teachers receive support (cf. 1 Timothy 5:17-18: elders worthy of double honor) but that they have made financial gain the motivating goal of their teaching.
6: Estin de porizmos megas hē eusebeia meta autarkeias (but godliness with contentment/self-sufficiency is great gain): the letter reverses the false teachers' equation. Autarkeia (contentment, self-sufficiency) — a core Stoic philosophical virtue: the wise person who is autarkēs is independent of external circumstances, sufficient within themselves through cultivated virtue. Paul adopts the Stoic vocabulary but regrounds it: godliness (relation to God) rather than inner virtue is the source of true sufficiency. The Stoic goal of autarkeia is fulfilled not by philosophical training but by godliness (cf. Philippians 4:11: Paul has 'learned' autarkein through union with Christ).
7: Ouden gar eisēnegkamen eis ton kosmon, hoti ouden dynametha kai exenegkein (for we brought nothing into the world, and we are unable to take anything out of it): the universally recognized truth about human mortality and possessionlessness. The closest parallels are Job 1:21 ('naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will depart') and Ecclesiastes 5:15 ('as they came from their mother's womb, so they will depart'). The maxim grounds the sufficiency-with-godliness teaching in the ontological reality of human existence: accumulation has no ultimate future.
8: Echontes de diatrophas kai skepas, toutois arskesthēsometha (but having food and clothing, we will be content with these): diatrophas (food, sustenance) and skepas (covering, shelter/clothing) — the two basic physical necessities. The pairing echoes Stoic and Cynic diatribes on the minimal requirements for a life of virtue, and also the wisdom tradition (Proverbs 30:8: 'Give me neither poverty nor riches'). The conditional minimal sufficiency — if we have these, we will be content — is the practical application of autarkeia grounded in godliness.
9:
Hoi de boulomenoi ploutein empipto
usin eis peirasmon kai pagida (those who want to get rich fall into temptation and a trap): the participial phrase boulomenoi ploutein (wanting to get rich) identifies the posture of wealth-craving, not wealth itself, as the danger. Peirasmon kai pagida (temptation and trap) — two images: the internal testing that pulls toward what destroys, and the external snare of the trapper. Epithumias pollas anōētous kai blaberas, hai bythi
zousi tous anthrōpous eis olethron kai apōleian (many foolish and harmful desires that plunge people into ruin and destruction) — bythizousi (to plunge/sink, literally submerge) is a nautical term for sinking a ship.
10: Rhiza gar pantōn tōn kakōn estin hē philarguria (for a root of all the evils is the love of money): philarguria (love of money/silver; philos + argyros) — the Pastoral Epistles' formulation is often misquoted as 'money is the root of all evil'; the actual claim is that love of money is a root of all kinds of evil (not the root of all evil without qualification). The Greco-Roman moralist tradition had similar formulations. Hēs tines oregomenoi (craving which some have) — oregomai (to reach out for, crave) is a different verb from epithumia (desire), suggesting the active reaching-out for what one does not yet possess. Apeplanethesan apo tēs pisteōs kai heautous periepeiran odynais pollais (have wandered from the faith and pierced themselves with many pains) — periepeiran (pierced through, impaled) is a vivid self-inflicted wound image.
11: Sy de, ō anthrōpe theou, tauta pheuge (but you, O man of God, flee these things): anthrōpe theou (man of God) — an OT prophetic title applied to Moses (Deuteronomy 33:1), Samuel (1 Samuel 9:6-8), Elijah (1 Kings 17:18, 24), Elisha (2 Kings 4:9), and other prophets; its application to Timothy elevates him to the prophetic tradition of covenant loyalty and counter-cultural faithfulness. Diōke de dikaiosynēn, eusebeian, pistin, agapēn, hypomonēn, prautēta (pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, endurance, gentleness) — the six-term virtue list balances the flight from vice (1 Timothy 6:11a) with the pursuit of virtue; diōkō (pursue) is the same verb used for persecution, implying active energetic pursuit.
12: Agōnizou ton kalon agōna tēs pisteōs (fight/contend the good contest/fight of faith): the athletic and military contest vocabulary (agōnizomai, agōn) is concentrated in the Pastoral Epistles and elsewhere in Paul (Colossians 1:29; 1 Timothy 4:12; Philippians 1:30; 1 Timothy 2:16; 1 Corinthians 9:25-27). Epilaboū tēs aiōniou zōēs, eis hēn eklēthēs kai hōmologēsas tēn kalēn homologian enōpion pollōn martyrōn (take hold of eternal life, to which you were called and confessed the good confession before many witnesses): homologian (confession, declaration of agreement) — probably a baptismal or ordination confession; enōpion pollōn martyrōn (before many witnesses) suggests a public liturgical context.
13: Parangellō soi enōpion tou theou tou zōogonountos ta panta kai Christou Iēsou tou martyrēsantos epi Pontiou Pilatou tēn kalēn homologian (I charge you before God who gives life to all things and Christ Jesus who testified the good confession before Pontius Pilate): zōogonountos ta panta (who gives life to all things) — the Creator-God as the ground of the solemn charge; the one who gives all life is the witness. Pontius Pilate (epi Pontiou Pilatou) — one of the rare explicit historical datings in the Pastoral Epistles (26-36 CE); Christ's testimony before Pilate (martyrēsas tēn kalēn homologian) is the paradigm for Timothy's public confession. The parallel between Christ's testimony before Pilate and Timothy's confession before witnesses (1 Timothy 6:12) is structurally deliberate.
14: Tērēsai se tēn entolēn aspilon anepilēmpton mechri tēs epiphaneias tou Kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou (to keep the command without spot and unimpeachable until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ): tēn entolēn (the commandment) — the singular may refer to the whole body of apostolic instruction or specifically the charge given to Timothy. Aspilon anepilēmpton (without spot, unimpeachable) — the dual compound is near-liturgical; the language echoes sacrificial purity requirements (amōmos = without blemish, Hebrews 9:14) and judicial acquittal language. Epiphaneia (appearing) — the Pastoral Epistles' distinctive Christological term (2 Timothy 1:10; 1 Timothy 4:1, 8; Titus 2:13; cf. 2 Thessalonians 2:8), used for both the first coming (2 Timothy 1:10) and the Parousia. It is also the Hellenistic technical term for the manifestation of a deity or the public appearance of a king.
16: Ho monos echōn athanasian, phōs oikōn aprositon, hon eiden oudeis anthrōpōn oude idein dynatai (who alone has immortality, dwelling in unapproachable light, whom no human has seen or is able to see): monos echōn athanasian (who alone has immortality) — God's immortality (athanasia) is underived and intrinsic, not shared by creation; the angels and humans who participate in immortality do so derivatively. Phōs oikōn aprositon (dwelling in unapproachable light) — combines the OT theophanies of the divine glory as fire and light (Exodus 24:17: consuming fire; Psalms 104:2: wrapped in light as a garment; 1 Kings 8:12: YHWH dwelling in thick darkness) with the Hellenistic philosophical tradition of the divine as pure light. Hon eiden oudeis anthrōpōn (whom no human has seen) — Exodus 33:20 (no one may see me and live); John 1:18 (no one has seen God at any time).
17: Tois plousiois en tō nyn aiōni (those who are rich in the present age): en tō nyn aiōni (in the present age) — the eschatological qualifier frames earthly wealth as age-specific: it belongs to the current order of things, which is passing. Hypeorphroneō (to be arrogant/think too highly of oneself) and ēlpikenas epi ploutou adēlotēti (having set hope on the uncertainty of wealth) — two linked dangers: the pride that wealth generates and the misplaced security it appears to offer. Adēlotēs ploutou (uncertainty/instability of wealth) — wealth as a fundamentally unreliable foundation for hope.
18: Agathoergein, ploutein en ergois kalois, eumetadotous einai, koinōnikous (to do good, to be rich in good works, to be generous, to be willing to share): the four-fold instruction redirects the wealthy from passive possession to active generosity. Koinōnikous (willing to share, generous in sharing) — from koinōnia (fellowship, sharing): the wealthy are to practice the community-partnership that is the letter's social ideal. Eumetadotous (generous, liberal in giving) — eu (well) + metadidōmi (share out); the compounding emphasizes ready and generous sharing, not grudging donation.
19:
Apothēsaurizonta
s heautois themelion kalon eis to mellon, hina epilabōntai tēs ontōs zōēs (storing up for themselves a good foundation for the future, so that they may take hold of the life that is really life): apothēsaurizō (to store up as treasure) — the same root as thēsauros (treasure); the wealthy who invest in good works are making an eschatological deposit. Tēs ontōs zōēs (the life that really is life) — the genitive of emphasis: authentic life, contrasted with the mere biological existence of the present age. The 'taking hold' (epilabōntai) mirrors the same verb applied to Timothy in 1 Timothy 6:12 (take hold of eternal life): the same eschatological grasp is available to those who invest in the kingdom rather than hoarding temporal wealth.
20: Ō Timothee, tēn parathēkēn phylaxon (O Timothy, guard the deposit): parathēkē (deposit entrusted for safekeeping) — a banking and legal term: the deposit made with a trusted party who is responsible for returning it intact. The apostolic teaching-tradition is a deposit entrusted to Timothy; he is the custodian, not the owner. Tas bebēlous kenophōnias kai antitheseis tēs pseudōnymou gnōseōs (godless empty chatter and the opposing arguments of what is falsely named knowledge): pseudōnymos (falsely named) applied to gnōsis (knowledge) — the false teachers' system is called 'knowledge' but is falsely labeled; it lacks true knowledge. Whether this is proto-Gnosticism or a local form of Jewish esoteric speculation, the epistemological claim is refuted by the label itself.
21: Hēn tines epangellomenoi peri tēn pistin ēstochēsan (which some professing have missed the mark regarding the faith): ēstochēsan (missed the mark, erred) — from astocheō (to miss the target); same verb as 1 Timothy 1:6 (having turned aside) and 2 Timothy 2:18 (who have swerved from the truth). The closing hē charis meth' hymōn (grace be with you) — the standard Pauline letter-closing formula, here in the plural 'you' (hymōn), indicating the letter's address extends beyond Timothy to the broader congregation at Ephesus.
